claim_chain: Neural indicators predict cognition but their role in cognition-mental health link is unknown
We quantified how neural indicators explain cognition-mental health covariation
Neuroimaging captures up to 48% of cognition-mental health covariation when multimodal
Predictive ability of neural indicators extends beyond cognition to capture shared variance with mental health
Findings validate brain-based markers as core units for cognitive functioning in mental health research

typical_pattern: Provide quantitative evidence or effect size
Compare to prior literature or expectations

likely_priorities: Quantitative validation of neural markers against behavioral outcomes
Comparison of predictive performance across MRI modalities
Methodological rigor in machine learning and cross-validation
Acknowledgment of unexplained variance and missing modalities
likely_attacks: Limited generalizability due to UK Biobank-specific characteristics
Missing neuroimaging modalities like task-based fMRI and PET
Measurement error and intra-individual variability between cognitive assessments and MRI
Potential confounding by socioeconomic status in mental health measures
Unexplained variance in cognition-mental health relationship
Specificity of findings to normative non-clinical samples
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly discusses limitations including: absence of certain neuroimaging modalities in UK Biobank (task-based fMRI, PET, ASL, MEG/EEG), measurement error and intra-individual variability, the fact that MRI represents only one level of neurobiological analysis per RDoC framework, and that findings may be specific to UK Biobank cohort characteristics. The authors also note that the mental health-cognition association may be bounded by other functional domains in normative samples, and that mental distress measures may reflect healthcare-seeking behavior influenced by socioeconomic status rather than distress per se.

examples.quoted_phrase: Our study is the first to quantify the contribution of the neural indicators of cognition
We demonstrate that the predictive ability of neural indicators extends beyond the prediction of cognition itself
may serve as a methodological 'bridge' linking other units of analysis
The remaining unexplained variance in the relationship between cognition and mental health likely stems from multiple sources
a pattern of RSFC maintained for an extended period may eventually cause concurrent alterations in cognition and mental health

phrase_evidence.phrase: Our study is the first to quantify the contribution of the neural indicators of cognition
We show that the performance of each neural indicator in predicting cognition per se is strongly related to its ability to explain the link between cognition and mental health
In other words, the 'robustness' of the neural indicator of cognition is associated with its capacity to capture the covariation between cognition and mental health
We further demonstrate that information from 72 neuroimaging phenotypes from three MRI modalities accounts for almost half of the variance
Integrating information from neuroimaging phenotypes within and across MRI modalities enhanced both the prediction of cognition and the explanation of the relationship
consistent with prior studies
aligns with existing literature
Although the observed mental health-cognition association is lower than within-sample estimates
This means that neural indicators that capture more of the individual differences in cognition also capture more of the cognition–mental health covariation
may indicate greater susceptibility to or exaggerated perception of stressful events
these findings may be specific to the UK Biobank cohort
Mixed evidence on the link between alcohol and drug use and cognition makes it difficult to draw definite conclusions
The remaining unexplained variance in the relationship between cognition and mental health likely stems from multiple sources
One possibility is the absence of certain neuroimaging modalities in the UK Biobank dataset
Moreover, there are inherent limitations in using MRI as a proxy for brain structure and function
Measurement error and intra-individual variability, such as differences in a cognitive state between cognitive assessments and MRI acquisition, may also contribute to the unexplained variance
Our findings validate brain-based neural markers as a core unit of analysis for cognitive functioning
we support interventions that enhance cognition as a pathway to improving mental health
Such work could enable personalised interventions, using neuroimaging to track cognitive changes and treatment efficacy
In line with the National Institute of Mental Health's RDoC framework
we shed light on the relationship between cognition and mental health as one of the six transdiagnostic spectrums
This may serve as a methodological 'bridge' linking other units of analysis
Although recent debates have challenged the predictive utility of MRI for cognition
our multimodal marker integrating 72 neuroimaging phenotypes captures nearly half of the mental health-explained variance in cognition
none of the neuroimaging phenotypes or MRI modalities alone is sufficient to provide a complete picture
combining all sources of information about brain structure and function reflected in three MRI modalities allows us to derive robust quantitative neuromarkers
Bootstrap distribution of Pearson r for the g-factor derived from Exploratory structural equation modeling
For visualization purposes, we display the results

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included. The profile is based solely on the provided discussion, conclusion, abstract, and figure legend sections.
